reason: The audit-base (upstream) version is strictly MORE hardened than main on two independent security axes, and main carries no countervailing OSS-only security property upstream lacks. (1) Upstream adds a whole MFA privileged-access gate (NIST IA-2(1)/(2) + IA-11): evaluatePrivilegedMfa / tokenHasMfaClaim / isAuthFreshForSensitiveOp / getMfaFreshnessSeconds + the MFA_AMR_VALUES set + acrSignalsMfa — fail-closed MFA-claim verification and auth-freshness checks that main has zero of. (2) Upstream fixes a real PII + token-internals prod-stdout leak: main logs raw token claims (alg/kid/iss/aud/appid/ver/tid), user emails, group GUIDs, the external-admin email allowlist, and admin status via console.log at DEFAULT level on EVERY token validation; upstream gates all of it behind AUTH_DEBUG via authDebug/authDebugError/authDebugWarn. A grep of main found no hardcoded admin email/IP/personal-data default that the sync would re-introduce. We WANT a future sync/port to bring upstream's version.

The only security-material difference is an O(N) bcrypt CPU-exhaustion DoS guard in validateApiKey, and the audit-base (upstream) is the side that HAS it while main REGRESSES to the unbounded scan. Upstream narrows to one candidate row via an indexed deterministic sha256 lookup_hash and runs bcrypt.compare exactly once (with a bounded, draining legacy-backfill path); main does findMany over ALL active keys and bcrypt.compares every one in a loop — a trivial DoS with many keys. We WANT a future sync to bring upstream's hardened version, so this must NOT be preserved. The awc_ vs oa_/oa_sys_ prefix difference is pure brand-rename cosmetics with no security delta.

The audit-base (upstream) version is strictly MORE hardened than main: it fails CLOSED on missing/placeholder required secrets in production (throws [FATAL], aborts boot) to prevent forged-token / insecure-boot, whereas main fails OPEN — it always generates an ephemeral runtime secret even in prod and explicitly boasts "ensures the API server always starts." We WANT a future sync to bring upstream's fail-closed behavior, so this must NOT be added to PRESERVE.

The audit-base (upstream) version is strictly stronger on auth/authz than main across three axes, and main has no OSS-only hardening upstream lacks. Upstream closes a fail-OPEN internal-service-auth bypass (whitespace-only INTERNAL_SERVICE_SECRET), adds session expiry/revocation + account-lockout enforcement, and adds a privileged-session MFA freshness gate — all of which main lacks. We WANT a future sync to bring these in, so this must NOT be on the PRESERVE list.

The audit-base (upstream) version is the WRITE side of an anti-DoS auth hardening that main entirely LACKS: it computes and stores a deterministic sha256 lookup_hash so validateApiKey() fetches at most one candidate row before bcrypt, instead of bcrypt-comparing the presented key against every active key (the O(N) bcrypt algorithmic-complexity DoS). main's version stores no lookup_hash and grep confirms the column/field exists nowhere in main's api source. A future sync SHOULD bring upstream's stronger version (plus the matching tokenValidator + DoS test); do NOT preserve. Remaining deltas are pure brand renames (awc_->oa_ prefix, base64url vs hex encoding of the SAME 32 random bytes / 256 bits of entropy — no entropy loss) and (request as any) typing churn, none of which are a security delta.

Both versions ship the same security posture: the admin preHandler guard (user?.isAdmin || role==='admin'), per-mutation admin_audit_log writes (admin_user_id/email/ip), and identical input validation. No hardcoded admin email/IP/secret/PII default, SSRF guard, or fail-closed behavior exists on main that the upstream base lacks. The only material delta is upstream splitting the READ-ONLY kill-switch into a separate adminReadOnlyModeRoutes plugin that carries its OWN admin preHandler guard AND still audits the toggle change — upstream's comment notes the split fixes a prior path where the inline toggle skipped writeAudit. So upstream is equal-or-better on audit-immutability; nothing OSS-specific must be preserved.

The audit-base (upstream) version has strictly STRONGER auth hardening than main and closes a real auth hole, so we WANT a future sync to bring it in. Upstream gates every route with the unified authMiddleware (signature-verified Azure RS256/JWKS + Google + local HS256 + api-key, with session revocation enforcement), whereas main uses a local HS256-only jwt.verify(token, JWT_SECRET) helper that cannot verify real SSO/RS256 tokens and ignores session revocation. Most importantly, main's POST /validate endpoint never calls getUserFromToken and is therefore completely UNAUTHENTICATED; upstream's onRequest authMiddleware now gates it. PRESERVE would clobber a security upgrade with the weaker, regressed version — the opposite of what we want.

The audit-base (upstream) version is a strict superset of main: it keeps every security property main has (the #789 MFA-freshness isMfaStale check, JWT validation, service-principal handling, fail-closed treating invalid tokens as expired) and ADDS strictly stronger auth hardening that main lacks — most notably a NIST IA-2 MFA-bypass guard in the refresh path. Main has no OSS-specific protection upstream would destroy; the only '-' diff line is trailing-whitespace churn. A future sync should bring upstream's stronger version, so this file must NOT be PRESERVEd.

Upstream (audit-base) adds an AC-2/AU-2 token-lifecycle audit trail that main lacks: an injectable AdminAuditSink that records every inter-service token mint/refresh in AdminAuditLog using a non-reversible truncated SHA-256 token_fingerprint and a has_secret boolean — never persisting the raw token or the shared secret. It is opt-in, best-effort (swallows sink failures), and byte-compatible with existing 1-arg call sites, so the returned token and the fail-closed-on-empty-secret behavior are unchanged. The core HMAC-SHA256 mint and empty-secret→prefix-only (proxy rejects) logic are identical on both sides. The only other delta is the oa_sys_ vs awc_system_ prefix, which is a pure brand rename (awc→oa) the sync rewrites automatically, so preserving main is not needed to keep proxy auth working. Upstream is strictly the stronger security version — we WANT a future sync to bring its audit instrumentation in.

The audit-base (upstream) version is strictly MORE hardened than main on the authz/HITL axis with zero regression. After ignoring brand/path/comment churn, EVERY non-cosmetic delta is upstream ADDING security that main lacks: (1) a synth chat-path risk gate (classifySynth) that DENIES prohibited-category synth and forces HITL on write/cloud-mod synth — main has no such gate, so its glob-allow-listed `synth`/`synth_execute` chat call auto-executes with NO SynthAbuseClassifier check; (2) a k8s protected-namespace HITL gate that forces a reason-required CRITICAL approval on mutations to protected/cluster-scoped namespaces even when a broad k8s_* glob would auto-allow; (3) an env-tunable HITL approval window (main hardcodes an unusably-short 120s that auto-denies before a human can respond); (4) a late-approve expiry-grace map. The only lines main has that base lacks are the WEAKER originals of these (the 120s literal, the un-gated rule resolver, the generic timeout). Main protects NO security property the upstream destroys. The upstream's new imports (SynthAbuseClassifier, CapabilityClassifier) already exist on the audit base, so a future sync brings the whole hardened bundle coherently. Do NOT add to PRESERVE — we WANT the sync/port to bring this stronger version. A test file caveat does not apply; this is production source.

This file is a thin re-export shim; the actual ACL authz decision logic lives in services/shared/workflow-engine/src/secretAcl.ts, which is byte-for-byte identical on both main and audit-base. The only delta in the shim is that upstream additionally re-exports the `AclDecision` result type (a strict superset of main's exports). There is no security/secret/PII/authz property main protects that upstream regresses — upstream is equal-or-better (larger, harmless type surface), so a future sync should be allowed to bring it.

Both files exist and differ only in auth hardening, all on the upstream side. The audit-base adds a NIST AC-7 failed-login lockout (Redis brute-force counter + account locking + LOGIN_FAILURE audit) that main entirely lacks, AND fail-closes the token-refresh path where main still mints fake random tokens and persists them as if valid. Upstream is strictly more secure; main has zero OSS-only security property that upstream regresses, so a future sync SHOULD bring upstream's version. Do NOT preserve.

The audit-base (upstream) version is a strict security superset of main, with no regression and no OSS-only hardening that main uniquely protects. Both sides enforce the secret ACL identically (fail-closed: on deny, resolveSecretValue returns null and writes a secret.acl_denied audit row), require WORKFLOW_SECRET_KEY (throws if unset), sanitize on output, and ship no hardcoded admin email / IP / PII. Upstream ADDS: (1) richer denial audit metadata (reason+details recorded on every acl_denied event), and (2) a new documented two-phase resolveSecretForExecution() that carries ACL metadata forward to interpolation-time enforcement rather than bypassing it. A future sync should bring the upstream version; do not add this path to PRESERVE.

The audit-base (upstream) version adds a fail-closed-in-production secrets guard that main lacks: it makes the bootstrap step critical in production and re-throws on a missing/invalid required secret so runStartup process.exit(1)s, preventing boot with an ephemeral/placeholder JWT signing secret (forgeable tokens). Dev/test stays lenient on both sides, so CI/local boot is unaffected. Main is strictly weaker (always warn-and-continue), so we WANT a future sync to bring upstream's stronger version — do not preserve.

The audit-base (upstream) version is a strict superset of main: lines 1-514 are byte-for-byte identical (no brand/security delta), and upstream appends 68 lines adding a brand-new test class TestForceReindexAuthHardening that main lacks. Those added tests assert STRONGER auth hardening — that POST /internal/reindex-tools is fail-CLOSED (rejects when INTERNAL_SERVICE_SECRET is unset/empty instead of the old fail-OPEN `if expected_secret:` that skipped auth) and uses constant-time hmac.compare_digest (CWE-208 timing-leak fix). Main asserts no OSS-only invariant that upstream regresses, so we WANT a future sync to bring the stronger upstream version. Preserving main would block these extra security regression tests.

The audit-base (upstream) version compares the shared internal service key with a constant-time safeEqual (sha256 + crypto.timingSafeEqual), while main regresses to a plain `===` that short-circuits and leaks the key length/prefix via a timing side-channel. Upstream is strictly stronger on the auth axis, so a future sync SHOULD bring it. Caveat: main also carries an OSS-only compat fix (accepting both x-agent-proxy and legacy x-openagentic-proxy markers) that a blind take would drop — but that header is a non-secret public marker, not a security property, so it does not justify PRESERVE; re-apply the dual-header accept on top of upstream's timing-safe compare.

Both versions exist and every security property in main is present byte-for-byte in upstream: the placeholder-secret rejection in getRuntimeConfig, the three @deprecated getApiKey/getFrontendSecret/getSigningSecret with DEV-only warnings, the SSO-only-default-off isLocalLoginEnabled, and the prod-disabled getRuntimeConfigObject. The ONLY substantive delta is upstream ADDING getIdpRuntimeConfig() — a raw read-through of the four IdP flags feeding a filterEnabledIdps selector (passes values un-defaulted so "unset vs disabled" is decided explicitly). That is an authz/login-surface refinement, not a regression: it removes no guard, adds no hardcoded admin-email/IP/PII, and weakens no secret handling. Main protects nothing upstream destroys, so do NOT preserve — a future sync bringing the upstream version (with the extra IdP selector) is desirable.

Both sides ship the same secret-redaction DLP feature, but upstream (audit-base) hardened the core algorithm: main redacts via iterative split(value).join('[redacted:name]') which re-scans already-emitted placeholder text and can corrupt placeholders, while upstream added a single-pass left-to-right scan (redactStringWithReplacements) that scans only the ORIGINAL input with greedy longest-first non-overlapping matches, so a later/shorter secret value cannot corrupt a placeholder emitted by an earlier secret. Upstream is strictly stronger DLP redaction; main protects no OSS-only secret/PII/auth invariant that upstream destroys. We want a future sync to bring upstream's version.
